transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hi Fiona, it's nice to meet you. I’m here to listen and support you. What feels most important for you to talk about today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Hi, nice to meet you too. I guess I've been feeling a bit overwhelmed lately. There’s just a lot going on, and I’m not sure where to start. I’ve been trying to manage my own stuff, but I feel like I’m letting people down, especially my friend Jersey. It’s just... hard to balance everything, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’re carrying a lot right now, and it’s understandable to feel overwhelmed with everything on your plate. You mentioned feeling like you might be letting your friend Jersey down—can you share a bit more about that? What’s been happening that makes you feel this way?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I guess it’s just that Jersey reached out to me recently, and I could tell she was looking for support. But I’ve been so caught up in my own issues that I couldn’t really be there for her. I keep thinking that she must be disappointed in me. It’s like I want to help, but I just feel... stuck. I don’t want to let her down, but I also don’t know how to manage my own feelings right now.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you really care about Jersey and want to be there for her, but you’re also feeling weighed down by your own struggles. That feeling of being stuck can be really tough. When you think about not being able to support her, what emotions come up for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I feel really sad and lonely, to be honest. There’s this heaviness that just kind of lingers. I also feel guilty, like I should be doing more. It’s frustrating because I want to be a good friend, but I’m just... not in a good place myself. It’s like I’m torn between wanting to help her and dealing with my own stuff. I guess that makes me feel even more isolated.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you for sharing that, Fiona. It sounds like you’re experiencing a mix of sadness, guilt, and frustration, which is really understandable given the situation. Feeling isolated while wanting to support someone else can be incredibly challenging. When you think about this heaviness and isolation, do you notice any specific thoughts or beliefs that come up for you about yourself or your role as a friend?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, I think I often feel like I should always be there for others, like it’s my responsibility to support them no matter what. When I can’t do that, I start to believe that maybe I’m not a good friend or that I’m failing somehow. It’s like this pressure I put on myself to be strong and helpful, but when I’m struggling, I feel like I’m letting everyone down. I guess it makes me question my worth in those relationships.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you hold a strong belief that being a good friend means always being available and supportive, which can create a lot of pressure for you. When you’re unable to meet that expectation, it leads to feelings of failure and questioning your worth in those relationships. That’s a heavy burden to carry. Can you recall any experiences or messages from your past that might have shaped this belief about needing to be strong for others?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think it’s just something I’ve always felt, maybe from a young age. I grew up in an environment where helping others was really emphasized. I remember feeling like I had to take care of my friends and family, and if I didn’t, it felt like I was letting them down. It’s like I internalized this idea that my value comes from how much I can do for others. So now, when I can’t help, it just reinforces that feeling of being undesirable or unwanted. It’s hard to shake off.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you’ve internalized a strong message from your upbringing about the importance of helping others, which has shaped how you view your own worth. That sense of needing to take care of others to feel valuable can be really challenging, especially when you’re struggling yourself. It’s understandable that this would create a cycle of feeling undesirable when you can’t meet those expectations. How do you feel about recognizing this connection between your past experiences and your current feelings?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It’s a bit of a relief, honestly, to see that connection. It helps me understand why I feel the way I do, but it’s also kind of daunting. I guess I’ve never really thought about how those early messages have shaped my beliefs about myself. It’s like I’m realizing that I’ve been holding myself to this impossible standard. I want to be there for my friends, but I also need to be kinder to myself. It’s just hard to break that cycle, you know?

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s great to hear that recognizing that connection brings you some relief, even if it feels daunting. Understanding how those early messages have influenced your beliefs is an important step. It sounds like you’re beginning to see the need for self-compassion alongside your desire to support your friends, which is a valuable insight. Breaking that cycle can be challenging, but it’s also a journey. What do you think might be a small step you could take toward being kinder to yourself in this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe I could start by allowing myself to take a break when I need it, instead of pushing myself to be available all the time. It feels hard to even think about that, but maybe I could set aside some time just for me, to recharge or do something I enjoy. I guess it’s about recognizing that it’s okay to not be perfect and that I don’t have to carry everything alone. It’s just a little scary to think about prioritizing my own needs.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really thoughtful and compassionate step, Fiona. Allowing yourself to take breaks and prioritize your own needs is an important part of self-care, even if it feels scary. It’s understandable to feel apprehensive about shifting your focus to yourself, especially when you’ve been conditioned to prioritize others. What do you think might help you feel more comfortable with taking that time for yourself?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think maybe reminding myself that it’s okay to take care of myself would help. I could try to challenge those thoughts that say I’m being selfish for wanting some time alone. Maybe I could even write down some positive affirmations or reasons why it’s important to take care of myself. It might also help to talk to someone I trust about it, just to get some support and encouragement. I guess I just need to find ways to validate my own needs without feeling guilty about it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those sound like wonderful strategies, Fiona. Writing down affirmations and reasons for self-care can be a powerful way to challenge those guilt-driven thoughts. Talking to someone you trust for support can also provide a sense of validation and encouragement as you navigate this process. It’s clear that you’re starting to recognize the importance of your own needs, and that’s a significant step. How do you feel about sharing these ideas with someone you trust? Do you think that would be a helpful next step for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think sharing my thoughts with someone I trust could be really helpful. It’s nice to have that support and to know I’m not alone in this. I feel a bit hesitant, though, because I worry about burdening them with my struggles. But I also know that opening up could help me feel less isolated. Maybe I could start by just mentioning how I’ve been feeling and see where the conversation goes. It feels like a step in the right direction, even if it’s a little scary.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It’s completely understandable to feel hesitant about sharing your struggles, especially when you’re concerned about burdening others. However, it sounds like you recognize the value of connection and support, which is a positive realization. Starting with how you’ve been feeling is a gentle way to open up the conversation, and it can help you gauge their willingness to listen. It’s okay to take small steps at your own pace. How do you feel about setting a time to have that conversation?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think setting a time to have that conversation could be a good idea. It would give me something to look forward to and prepare for. Maybe I could reach out to my friend and suggest getting together for coffee or a chat. I feel a mix of nervousness and hope about it, but I think it’s important to take that step. I want to feel more connected and supported, and this could be a way to start. I’ll try to set a time soon.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a positive plan, Fiona. Reaching out to your friend for coffee or a chat is a great way to create that space for connection and support. It’s completely normal to feel a mix of nervousness and hope, especially when you’re taking a step toward opening up. It shows that you’re valuing your own needs and seeking connection, which is really important. When you think about this upcoming conversation, what are you most hopeful for?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m hopeful that my friend will be understanding and supportive. I really want to feel heard and validated, and maybe even get some perspective on what I’m going through. I hope that sharing my feelings will help lighten the load a bit, and that it’ll remind me that I’m not alone in this. I guess I’m also hopeful that it’ll encourage me to keep being honest about my needs moving forward. It feels like a small step, but it could lead to something really meaningful.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful perspective, Fiona. It sounds like you’re looking for understanding and validation, which are such important aspects of feeling connected. Sharing your feelings could indeed lighten your emotional load and remind you of the support that’s available to you. It’s encouraging to hear that you see this as a meaningful step toward being more honest about your needs in the future. As you prepare for this conversation, how do you think you might remind yourself of your worth and the importance of your needs, regardless of the outcome?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think I could remind myself that my feelings are valid, no matter how the conversation goes. I might write down some affirmations about my worth and the importance of taking care of myself, just to have something to refer back to if I start feeling uncertain. It could also help to reflect on times when I’ve been there for others and how that doesn’t diminish my own needs. I guess I just need to keep reminding myself that it’s okay to prioritize myself, and that reaching out for support doesn’t make me any less valuable as a friend.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Those are wonderful strategies, Fiona. Acknowledging that your feelings are valid is a crucial part of this process, and having affirmations to refer back to can provide you with reassurance when you need it. Reflecting on your past experiences of supporting others while also recognizing your own needs is a powerful way to reinforce your worth. It’s clear that you’re committed to nurturing both yourself and your friendships, which is a healthy balance. As you move forward, what do you think will be the most important thing for you to remember about this journey?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think the most important thing for me to remember is that it’s okay to take my time and that it’s a process. I don’t have to have everything figured out right away. It’s about progress, not perfection. I want to remind myself that it’s okay to lean on others and that prioritizing my own needs doesn’t mean I’m failing as a friend. I guess I just need to be patient with myself and allow space for growth. It’s a journey, and I’m learning to navigate it one step at a time.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That’s a beautiful realization, Fiona. Emphasizing that it’s a process and allowing yourself the grace of patience is so important. Progress over perfection is a valuable mindset to carry with you as you navigate your feelings and relationships. It sounds like you’re cultivating a compassionate approach toward yourself, which will serve you well on this journey. As you take these steps, how do you feel about checking in with yourself regularly to see how you’re doing?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I think checking in with myself regularly could be really beneficial. It would give me a chance to reflect on how I’m feeling and whether I’m honoring my own needs. I could set aside a few moments each week to just pause and assess where I’m at emotionally. Maybe I could even journal about it to help clarify my thoughts. It feels like a good way to stay connected with myself and ensure that I’m not getting too caught up in trying to support others at the expense of my own well-being. I think it could help me stay grounded as I navigate this journey.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a fantastic plan, Fiona. Setting aside time each week to check in with yourself and reflect on your emotions can be a powerful way to stay connected to your needs. Journaling can also provide clarity and help you process your thoughts. It’s great that you’re thinking about how to maintain that balance between supporting others and taking care of yourself. As you embark on this journey, what are you most looking forward to discovering about yourself?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: I’m looking forward to discovering that it’s okay to be vulnerable and that I can still be a good friend while taking care of myself. I hope to learn more about what I truly need and what brings me joy, separate from my role in supporting others. I want to explore my own interests and passions without feeling guilty about it. I think this journey could help me build a stronger sense of self-worth that isn’t solely tied to how I help others. I’m excited to see how that might change my relationships and my overall well-being.
